requirements.question: Prepare the journal entry for Year 1 compensation expense.
Prepare the journal entry on December 31, Year 2 when the CEO forfeits all RSUs.
requirements.explanation:
**Journal Entry Solution:**

Reverse 2 years of expense: $142,500 × 2 = $285,000

**Key Concept:** Remember that debits must equal credits. Check that you've correctly identified whether each account increases or decreases.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: RSUs are measured at fair value on grant date
Compensation cost recognized ratably over service period
Actual forfeitures reverse previously recognized expense

tbs.title: Earnings Per Share - Complex Capital Structure
tbs.scenario:
Delta Industries reports the following for Year 1:
• Net income: $2,400,000
• Preferred dividends declared: $150,000 (non-convertible)
• Common shares outstanding January 1: 500,000
• New shares issued April 1: 100,000
• Treasury shares purchased July 1: 50,000
• 8% convertible bonds: $1,000,000 face value, convertible into 40,000 shares
• Stock options: 30,000 options with exercise price $25, average market price $40
• Tax rate: 25%

requirements.question: Calculate weighted-average common shares outstanding for basic EPS.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

500,000 × 12/12 + 100,000 × 9/12 - 50,000 × 6/12 = 500,000 + 75,000 - 25,000 = 550,000. Recheck: 500,000 (full year) + 100,000 × 9/12 (Apr-Dec) - 50,000 × 6/12 (Jul-Dec) = 500,000 + 75,000 - 25,000 = 550,000. Correction: should be 537,500

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate basic earnings per share.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Basic EPS = (Net income - Preferred dividends) / Weighted shares = ($2,400,000 - $150,000) / 537,500 = $4.19

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate incremental shares from stock options using treasury stock method.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Options proceeds = 30,000 × $25 = $750,000. Shares repurchased = $750,000 / $40 = 18,750. Incremental shares = 30,000 - 18,750 = 11,250

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate diluted earnings per share.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Diluted numerator = $2,250,000 + ($80,000 × 0.75 interest avoided) = $2,310,000. Diluted denominator = 537,500 + 11,250 + 40,000 = 588,750. Diluted EPS = $3.92

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Weight shares by months outstanding
Treasury stock method for options: (Market - Exercise) / Market × Options
If-converted method for bonds: Add back interest (after-tax) and shares

tbs.title: Deferred Tax Asset and Liability
tbs.scenario:
Titan Corp. has the following temporary differences at December 31, Year 1:

| Item | Book Basis | Tax Basis | Difference |
|------|------------|-----------|------------|
| Depreciation | $200,000 | $280,000 | ($80,000) |
| Warranty reserve | $0 | $45,000 | $45,000 |
| Prepaid rent income | $30,000 | $0 | $30,000 |
| Bad debt allowance | $0 | $25,000 | $25,000 |

Enacted tax rate: 21%
Beginning deferred tax asset: $15,000
Beginning deferred tax liability: $12,000
Taxable income for Year 1: $800,000

requirements.question: Calculate the ending deferred tax asset.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

DTA sources: Warranty $45,000 + Bad debt $25,000 = $70,000. DTA = $70,000 × 21% = $14,700

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the ending deferred tax liability.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

DTL sources: Depreciation $80,000 + Prepaid rent $30,000 = $110,000. DTL = $110,000 × 21% = $23,100

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate current income tax payable.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Current tax = Taxable income × Rate = $800,000 × 21% = $168,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate total income tax expense for Year 1.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Current tax $168,000 + Change in DTL ($23,100 - $12,000) - Change in DTA ($14,700 - $15,000) = $168,000 + $11,100 + $300 = $179,400

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: DTA = Future deductible amounts × Tax rate
DTL = Future taxable amounts × Tax rate
Tax expense = Current tax payable ± Changes in deferred taxes

tbs.title: Business Combination - Purchase Price Allocation
tbs.scenario:
On July 1, Year 1, Apex Corp. acquires 100% of Beta Inc. for $5,000,000 cash.

Beta's balance sheet at acquisition date:

| Asset/Liability | Book Value | Fair Value |
|-----------------|------------|------------|
| Cash | $200,000 | $200,000 |
| Accounts receivable | $400,000 | $380,000 |
| Inventory | $600,000 | $750,000 |
| Equipment | $1,200,000 | $1,500,000 |
| Patent (not recorded) | $0 | $400,000 |
| Accounts payable | ($300,000) | ($300,000) |
| Long-term debt | ($800,000) | ($850,000) |

Acquisition-related costs: $150,000

requirements.question: Calculate the fair value of net identifiable assets acquired.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

FV assets = $200,000 + $380,000 + $750,000 + $1,500,000 + $400,000 = $3,230,000. FV liabilities = $300,000 + $850,000 = $1,150,000. Net = $3,230,000 - $1,150,000 = $2,080,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the amount of goodwill from this acquisition.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Goodwill = Purchase price - FV of net assets = $5,000,000 - $2,080,000 = $2,920,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: How should the $150,000 acquisition costs be treated?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Acquisition-related costs are expensed as incurred under ASC 805. They are not capitalized as part of goodwill.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Prepare the journal entry to record the acquisition.

hints: Record assets at fair value, not book value
Recognize identifiable intangibles separately from goodwill
Acquisition costs are expensed, not capitalized

tbs.title: Pension Accounting - Defined Benefit Plan
tbs.scenario:
Nordic Corp. sponsors a defined benefit pension plan. The following information pertains to Year 1:

Beginning balances:
• Projected benefit obligation (PBO): $2,500,000
• Plan assets at fair value: $2,200,000

Year 1 transactions:
• Service cost: $180,000
• Interest cost (discount rate 5%): ?
• Expected return on plan assets: $154,000
• Actual return on plan assets: $175,000
• Employer contributions: $200,000
• Benefits paid: $150,000
• Actuarial loss on PBO: $45,000

requirements.question: Calculate the interest cost for Year 1.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Interest cost = Beginning PBO × Discount rate = $2,500,000 × 5% = $125,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the ending projected benefit obligation.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Ending PBO = Beginning $2,500,000 + Service cost $180,000 + Interest cost $125,000 + Actuarial loss $45,000 - Benefits paid $150,000 = $2,700,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the ending fair value of plan assets.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Ending assets = Beginning $2,200,000 + Actual return $175,000 + Contributions $200,000 - Benefits paid $150,000 = $2,425,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the pension expense for Year 1.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Pension expense = Service cost $180,000 + Interest cost $125,000 - Expected return $154,000 = $151,000 (Note: Actuarial gains/losses go to OCI)

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What is the funded status at year-end?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Funded status = Plan assets - PBO = $2,425,000 - $2,700,000 = ($275,000) underfunded

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Interest cost = Beginning PBO × Discount rate
Use EXPECTED return in pension expense, not actual
Difference between actual and expected return goes to OCI
Funded status = Plan assets - PBO

tbs.title: Convertible Debt - Induced Conversion
tbs.scenario:
On January 1, Year 1, Omni Corp. issued $2,000,000 of 5% convertible bonds at par. Each $1,000 bond is convertible into 20 shares of $1 par common stock.

On July 1, Year 3, to induce conversion, Omni offers bondholders 25 shares per bond (instead of 20) if they convert by July 31. All bondholders accept.

Market price of stock on conversion date: $55 per share
Carrying value of bonds at conversion: $2,000,000

requirements.question: How many shares will be issued under the induced conversion?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$2,000,000 / $1,000 = 2,000 bonds × 25 shares = 50,000 shares

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the conversion expense (fair value of extra shares).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Extra shares = 2,000 bonds × 5 extra shares = 10,000 shares. Expense = 10,000 × $55 = $550,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Prepare the journal entry to record the induced conversion.

hints: Extra shares are inducement - expense at fair value
Original shares convert at book value (no gain/loss)
APIC is the plug figure

tbs.title: Consolidation - Intercompany Inventory Profit
tbs.scenario:
Parent Corp. owns 100% of Sub Inc. During Year 1:
• Parent sold inventory to Sub for $500,000 (cost to Parent: $350,000)
• Sub sold 70% of this inventory to outside parties for $420,000
• Sub still holds 30% of inventory at year-end

Year 1 separate company data:
| | Parent | Sub | Combined |
|---|--------|-----|----------|
| Sales | $3,000,000 | $1,200,000 | $4,200,000 |
| Cost of goods sold | $1,800,000 | $850,000 | $2,650,000 |
| Gross profit | $1,200,000 | $350,000 | $1,550,000 |

requirements.question: Calculate the gross profit percentage on the intercompany sale.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Gross profit = ($500,000 - $350,000) / $500,000 = 30%

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the unrealized intercompany profit in ending inventory.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Inventory on hand = 30% × $500,000 = $150,000. Unrealized profit = $150,000 × 30% = $45,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What is consolidated sales for Year 1?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Combined $4,200,000 - Intercompany sale $500,000 = $3,700,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What is consolidated cost of goods sold for Year 1?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Combined COGS $2,650,000 - Intercompany purchases $500,000 + Unrealized profit $45,000 = $2,195,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What is consolidated gross profit for Year 1?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Consolidated sales $3,700,000 - Consolidated COGS $2,195,000 = $1,505,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Eliminate 100% of intercompany sales
Defer unrealized profit in ending inventory
Gross profit % = (Selling price - Cost) / Selling price

tbs.title: Statement of Cash Flows - Indirect Method
tbs.scenario:
Horizon Corp. provides the following Year 1 data:

Income Statement:
• Net income: $450,000
• Depreciation expense: $85,000
• Amortization of bond premium: $5,000
• Gain on sale of equipment: $15,000
• Loss on sale of investment: $8,000

Balance Sheet Changes:
• Accounts receivable increased: $35,000
• Inventory decreased: $20,000
• Prepaid expenses increased: $8,000
• Accounts payable decreased: $12,000
• Accrued liabilities increased: $18,000
• Income taxes payable decreased: $7,000

requirements.question: Calculate net cash provided by operating activities.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Start with net income $450,000. Add back: Depreciation $85,000, Loss on investment $8,000. Subtract: Gain on equipment $15,000, Bond premium amortization $5,000. Working capital: -A/R $35,000, +Inv $20,000, -Prepaid $8,000, -A/P $12,000, +Accrued $18,000, -Tax payable $7,000. Total = $499,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Which item reduces cash from operations when using the indirect method?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

An increase in accounts receivable means sales were made but cash not collected - reduces operating cash flow.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: How should the gain on equipment sale be reported?
options: Add to net income in operating section
Subtract from net income in operating section
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Gains are subtracted from net income to avoid double-counting. The full proceeds are reported in investing activities.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Add non-cash expenses (depreciation, amortization)
Remove gains/losses (reported in investing)
Asset increases = use of cash; Liability increases = source of cash

tbs.title: Asset Retirement Obligation
tbs.scenario:
On January 1, Year 1, Greenfield Energy places an offshore drilling platform in service. Estimated useful life: 20 years, no salvage value.

Asset retirement information:
• Estimated cost to remove platform in 20 years: $8,000,000
• Credit-adjusted risk-free rate: 6%
• Present value factor for 20 periods at 6%: 0.31180

The platform cost $50,000,000 to construct.

requirements.question: Calculate the initial asset retirement obligation (ARO).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

ARO = Future obligation × PV factor = $8,000,000 × 0.31180 = $2,494,400

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Prepare the journal entry to record the ARO at inception.
Calculate the total cost of the drilling platform to be depreciated.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Platform cost $50,000,000 + ARO asset $2,494,400 = $52,494,400

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the accretion expense for Year 1.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Accretion = ARO balance × Risk-free rate = $2,494,400 × 6% = $149,664

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the annual depreciation expense (straight-line).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Depreciation = $52,494,400 / 20 years = $2,624,720

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: ARO recorded at present value of future obligation
Capitalize ARO as part of asset cost
Accretion expense increases ARO over time (like interest)

tbs.title: Foreign Currency Translation
tbs.scenario:
US Parent owns 100% of Foreign Sub, whose functional currency is the Euro (€).

Foreign Sub's December 31, Year 1 balance sheet (in Euros):
• Cash: €100,000
• Inventory: €200,000 (acquired evenly throughout year)
• Equipment: €500,000 (acquired January 1, Year 1)
• Accumulated depreciation: €50,000
• Total assets: €750,000
• Accounts payable: €150,000
• Common stock: €400,000 (issued when rate was $1.20)
• Retained earnings: €200,000

Exchange rates:
• January 1, Year 1: $1.20/€
• Average for Year 1: $1.25/€
• December 31, Year 1: $1.30/€

requirements.question: Translate cash using the appropriate rate.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Monetary assets use current rate: €100,000 × $1.30 = $130,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Translate equipment (net) using the appropriate rate.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Equipment €500,000 - Accum depr €50,000 = €450,000. Translate at current rate: €450,000 × $1.30 = $585,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Translate common stock using the appropriate rate.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Common stock uses historical rate when issued: €400,000 × $1.20 = $480,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Where is the cumulative translation adjustment reported?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Translation adjustments for foreign subsidiaries with different functional currencies are reported in OCI, part of AOCI in stockholders equity.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Assets/Liabilities: Current rate
Common stock: Historical rate
Revenues/Expenses: Average rate
Translation adjustment goes to OCI (not P&L)

tbs.title: Segment Reporting - Quantitative Thresholds

requirements.question: Calculate the revenue threshold (10% test).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Revenue threshold = 10% × $800,000 = $80,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the profit/loss threshold (10% test - use greater of absolute values).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Total profits = $35,000 + $28,000 + $12,000 + $85,000 = $160,000. Total losses = $15,000 + $8,000 = $23,000. Greater = $160,000. Threshold = 10% × $160,000 = $16,000. Alternative: $137,000 net profit × 10% = $13,700

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Which segments are reportable based on the quantitative thresholds? (Select all that apply)
options: Segments A, C, and E only
Segments A, B, C, D, E, and F
Segments A, B, C, E only
Segments A, C, E, and F
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Revenue test ≥$80,000: A ($180k), C ($95k), E ($350k). Profit test: Use absolute value - A ($35k), E ($85k), C ($28k), B ($15k) meet threshold. Asset test ≥$220,000: A, B, C, E. Reportable = A, B, C, E.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Revenue test: 10% of combined revenue
Profit test: 10% of greater of (total profits, absolute total losses)
Asset test: 10% of combined assets
Meet ANY one test = reportable segment

tbs.title: Interim Reporting - Inventory Loss Recovery
tbs.scenario:
Quarterly inventory data for Midland Corp. (fiscal year = calendar year):

| Quarter | Cost | Market (NRV) |
|---------|------|--------------|
| Q1 | $500,000 | $450,000 |
| Q2 | $520,000 | $540,000 |
| Q3 | $480,000 | $510,000 |
| Q4 | $550,000 | $530,000 |

The company uses lower of cost or NRV. Any recoveries are limited to previously recognized losses.

requirements.question: What inventory loss is recognized in Q1?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Cost $500,000 > NRV $450,000. Write down $50,000.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What inventory adjustment is recognized in Q2?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Cost $520,000 < NRV $540,000. Recovery limited to prior loss = $50,000. But we can only recover what we expensed. Since this is new inventory, compare cost to market. NRV > cost, so no loss. Prior Q1 inventory was sold. No adjustment in Q2.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What is the inventory value to report at Q4 end?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Lower of cost ($550,000) or NRV ($530,000) = $530,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: How should interim inventory losses expected to be recovered by year-end be treated?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Under interim reporting guidance, if inventory losses are expected to be recovered by year-end, they should not be recognized in the interim period.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Apply LCM/LCNRV at each interim date
Expected year-end recoveries can defer interim losses
Recovery limited to previously recognized losses

tbs.title: Troubled Debt Restructuring - Modification
tbs.scenario:
Borrower Corp. owes Bank $1,000,000 on a 10% note due December 31, Year 1. Due to financial difficulties, the terms are modified on January 1, Year 2:

Modified terms:
• Principal reduced to $800,000
• Interest rate reduced to 6%
• Maturity extended to December 31, Year 4 (3 years)
• Accrued interest of $100,000 is forgiven

Bank's assessment: The restructuring represents a troubled debt restructuring under legacy GAAP.

PV factors at 10% (original rate):
• PV of $1 for 3 periods: 0.75132
• PV of ordinary annuity for 3 periods: 2.48685

requirements.question: Calculate the total future cash payments under modified terms.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Principal $800,000 + Interest ($800,000 × 6% × 3 years) = $800,000 + $144,000 = $944,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What is the carrying amount of debt before restructuring?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Principal $1,000,000 + Accrued interest $100,000 = $1,100,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Does the debtor recognize a gain on restructuring?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Carrying amount $1,100,000 > Future payments $944,000. Gain = $1,100,000 - $944,000 = $156,000. Under TDR rules, reduce liability to future cash flows and recognize gain.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Prepare the debtor's journal entry for the restructuring.

hints: Compare carrying amount to total future cash payments
If carrying amount > future payments, recognize gain
No interest expense recorded (effective rate = 0%)

tbs.title: Variable Interest Entity - Consolidation
tbs.scenario:
Alpha Corp. owns 30% of the equity in Special Purpose Entity (SPE). The SPE was created to lease equipment.

SPE structure:
• Total assets: $10,000,000 (equipment)
• Equity investment: $500,000 (Alpha: $150,000, Others: $350,000)
• Senior debt: $9,500,000 (held by Bank)

Variable interests:
• Alpha guarantees 80% of SPE's debt
• Alpha has a management contract that provides 50% of expected returns
• Alpha can direct the most significant activities (equipment purchases, lease terms)

Expected losses: $400,000
Expected residual returns: $600,000

requirements.question: Is the SPE a variable interest entity (VIE)?
options: Yes, equity at risk is insufficient
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Equity is $500,000 which is 5% of $10M assets. The 10% equity test and risk characteristics suggest VIE status. Equity holders lack substantive rights and don't absorb expected losses.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Alpha's share of expected losses through the guarantee.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Alpha guarantees 80% of debt. Expected losses × Alpha's share = $400,000 × 80% = $320,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Should Alpha consolidate the SPE?
options: Yes, Alpha is the primary beneficiary
No, Bank holds majority of debt
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Alpha (1) has power to direct activities affecting economic performance, and (2) has obligation to absorb losses (guarantee) and right to receive benefits (management fees). Alpha is primary beneficiary and must consolidate.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What amount of assets would Alpha report from consolidating the VIE?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Full consolidation = 100% of VIE's assets = $10,000,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: VIE test: Insufficient equity at risk, lack of control through equity
Primary beneficiary: Power + economic interest (losses or returns)
Consolidate 100% of VIE assets, recognize NCI if applicable

tbs.title: Equity Method - Intercompany Upstream Sale
tbs.scenario:
Investor Corp. owns 40% of Associate Inc. (equity method applies).

Year 1 data:
• Associate's net income: $800,000
• Dividends paid by Associate: $200,000
• Associate sold inventory to Investor for $300,000 (cost to Associate: $180,000)
• Investor sold 60% of this inventory to outside parties
• 40% remains in Investor's ending inventory

Beginning investment balance: $2,000,000

requirements.question: Calculate the gross profit on the intercompany sale.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Selling price $300,000 - Cost $180,000 = $120,000 gross profit

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate unrealized profit that must be deferred.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Unrealized portion = 40% unsold × $120,000 = $48,000. Investor's share = $48,000 × 40% = $19,200. Wait, for upstream, defer Investor's ownership % of unrealized profit in ending inventory.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Investor's share of Associate's earnings (equity income).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Investor share = 40% × $800,000 = $320,000. Less deferred profit: $48,000 × 40% = $19,200. Equity income = $320,000 - $19,200 = $300,800

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the ending investment balance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Beginning $2,000,000 + Equity income $300,800 - Dividends received ($200,000 × 40%) = $2,000,000 + $300,800 - $80,000 = $2,220,800

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Upstream sale: Associate sells to Investor
Defer investor's % of unrealized profit in investor's inventory
Investment = Beginning + Equity income - Dividends received

tbs.title: Hedging - Cash Flow Hedge of Forecasted Transaction
tbs.scenario:
On September 1, Year 1, Commodore Corp. anticipates purchasing 100,000 barrels of oil in March Year 2. To hedge against price increases, it enters into a forward contract to buy 100,000 barrels at $75 per barrel.

Key dates and spot prices:
• September 1, Year 1 (inception): Spot $72, Forward $75, Contract FV = $0
• December 31, Year 1 (year-end): Spot $80, Forward $82, Contract FV = $650,000
• March 1, Year 2 (settlement): Spot $85, Forward $85, Contract FV = $1,000,000

The hedge is designated as a cash flow hedge and is perfectly effective.

requirements.question: Prepare the journal entry on December 31, Year 1.
What is the change in OCI from December 31, Year 1 to March 1, Year 2?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Contract FV increased from $650,000 to $1,000,000 = $350,000 additional gain to OCI

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Prepare the entry to record the oil purchase at settlement (March 1, Year 2).
requirements.explanation:
**Journal Entry Solution:**

100,000 barrels × $85 spot = $8,500,000

**Key Concept:** Remember that debits must equal credits. Check that you've correctly identified whether each account increases or decreases.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: When the oil is sold, how is the AOCI balance reclassified?
requirements.explanation:
**Journal Entry Solution:**

Cash flow hedge gain in AOCI is reclassified to earnings (reducing COGS) when hedged item affects earnings.

**Key Concept:** Remember that debits must equal credits. Check that you've correctly identified whether each account increases or decreases.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Cash flow hedge: Effective portion to OCI
Reclassify from AOCI when hedged item affects earnings
Forward contract recorded at fair value

tbs.title: Software Development Costs
tbs.scenario:
TechStart Inc. is developing software for external sale. Project timeline and costs:

| Phase | Period | Costs |
|-------|--------|-------|
| Conceptual formulation | Jan-Feb Y1 | $150,000 |
| Design and planning | Mar-Apr Y1 | $200,000 |
| Coding and testing | May-Aug Y1 | $600,000 |
| Tech feasibility established | July 15, Y1 | - |
| Coding (post-feasibility) | Jul-Aug Y1 | $300,000 |
| Testing and debugging | Sep-Oct Y1 | $180,000 |
| Production masters | Nov Y1 | $50,000 |
| Product sales begin | Dec Y1 | - |

Expected useful life: 3 years
Expected revenue Year 1-3: $800,000, $1,200,000, $1,000,000

requirements.question: Calculate the total costs to expense as R&D.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Expenses before tech feasibility: Conceptual $150,000 + Design $200,000 + Coding (pre-July 15) approximately $300,000 = $650,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the total costs to capitalize.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Costs after tech feasibility: Post-feasibility coding $300,000 + Testing $180,000 + Production masters $50,000 = $530,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Year 2 amortization using greater of straight-line or revenue method.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Straight-line: $530,000 / 3 = $176,667. Revenue method: ($1,200,000 / $3,000,000) × $530,000 = $212,000. Use greater = $212,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Capitalize costs AFTER technological feasibility
Amortize using GREATER of straight-line or % revenue
Production masters are capitalizable

tbs.title: Nonmonetary Exchange - Commercial Substance
tbs.scenario:
RealCo exchanges equipment with TradeCo. The exchange has commercial substance.

RealCo's equipment:
• Original cost: $400,000
• Accumulated depreciation: $150,000
• Fair value: $300,000

TradeCo's equipment:
• Fair value: $320,000

RealCo pays $20,000 cash to TradeCo.

requirements.question: Calculate the book value of RealCo's equipment given up.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Book value = Cost - Accumulated depreciation = $400,000 - $150,000 = $250,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate RealCo's gain or loss on the exchange.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

FV of asset given ($300,000) - Book value ($250,000) = $50,000 gain

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: At what amount should RealCo record the new equipment?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

With commercial substance, record at FV of asset received = $320,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Prepare RealCo's journal entry for the exchange.

hints: Commercial substance: Record at fair value
Recognize full gain/loss
Cash paid added to cost of asset received

tbs.title: Change in Accounting Estimate - Depreciation
tbs.scenario:
On January 1, Year 1, Metro Corp. purchased machinery for $500,000 with an estimated useful life of 10 years and salvage value of $50,000. Straight-line depreciation is used.

On January 1, Year 4, Metro revises the total useful life to 8 years (from original date) and the salvage value to $20,000.

requirements.question: Calculate accumulated depreciation at December 31, Year 3.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Annual depreciation = ($500,000 - $50,000) / 10 = $45,000. After 3 years = $135,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the book value at January 1, Year 4.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Cost $500,000 - Accumulated depreciation $135,000 = $365,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the revised annual depreciation for Year 4 and beyond.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Remaining depreciable base = $365,000 - $20,000 = $345,000. Remaining life = 8 - 3 = 5 years. Annual depreciation = $345,000 / 5 = $69,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: How should the change in estimate be reported?
options: Prospectively, affecting current and future years
As a change in accounting principle
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Changes in estimate are applied prospectively. No restatement of prior periods.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Changes in estimate are prospective
Use remaining book value and remaining life
No restatement of prior periods

tbs.title: Contingent Liability - Probability Assessment
tbs.scenario:
Sterling Corp. is involved in three legal matters at December 31, Year 1:

Case A - Product Liability:
• Probable loss, attorneys estimate range of $500,000 to $900,000
• No amount in range is more likely than others

Case B - Patent Infringement:
• Reasonably possible loss of $1,200,000
• Company believes it has meritorious defense

Case C - Environmental Remediation:
• Remote possibility of loss
• Potential exposure: $2,500,000

Case D - Employee Discrimination:
• Probable loss, best estimate: $350,000

requirements.question: What amount should be accrued for Case A?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

When no amount in range is more likely, accrue the minimum of the range = $500,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What amount should be accrued for Case B?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Reasonably possible losses are disclosed but not accrued.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What is the total contingent loss accrual for Year 1?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Case A $500,000 + Case D $350,000 = $850,000 (Only probable and estimable are accrued)

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What disclosure is required for Case B?
options: Disclose nature and estimate of possible loss
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Reasonably possible losses must be disclosed, including nature and estimated amount or range.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Probable + Estimable = Accrue
Reasonably possible = Disclose only
Remote = No accrual or disclosure required
Range with no best estimate = Accrue minimum

tbs.title: Statement of Cash Flows - Indirect Method
tbs.scenario:
Riverside Corporation needs to prepare its statement of cash flows for the year ended December 31, Year 1, using the indirect method. The following information is available:

Income Statement Data:
• Net income: $450,000
• Depreciation expense: $85,000
• Amortization of patent: $12,000
• Gain on sale of equipment: $25,000
• Loss on sale of investment: $8,000

Balance Sheet Changes:
• Accounts receivable increased: $45,000
• Inventory decreased: $30,000
• Prepaid expenses increased: $5,000
• Accounts payable decreased: $20,000
• Accrued liabilities increased: $15,000
• Income taxes payable decreased: $10,000

Additional Information:
• Equipment with book value of $40,000 sold for $65,000
• New equipment purchased for $175,000
• Dividends paid: $100,000
• Proceeds from issuance of common stock: $200,000
• Repayment of long-term debt: $75,000

requirements.question: Calculate cash flows from operating activities using the indirect method.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Net income ($450,000) + Depreciation ($85,000) + Amortization ($12,000) - Gain on equipment ($25,000) + Loss on investment ($8,000) - AR increase ($45,000) + Inventory decrease ($30,000) - Prepaid increase ($5,000) - AP decrease ($20,000) + Accrued increase ($15,000) - Taxes payable decrease ($10,000) = $495,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate cash flows from investing activities.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Proceeds from equipment sale ($65,000) - Equipment purchased ($175,000) = -$110,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate cash flows from financing activities.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Stock issued ($200,000) - Dividends paid ($100,000) - Debt repayment ($75,000) = $25,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the net change in cash for the year.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Operating ($495,000) + Investing (-$110,000) + Financing ($25,000) = $410,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Which of the following is NOT an adjustment to net income in the operating section?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Dividends paid are a financing activity, not an adjustment to operating cash flows. Depreciation, gains/losses, and changes in working capital are all operating adjustments.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Add back non-cash expenses (depreciation, amortization)
Subtract gains, add back losses
Current asset increases = cash outflow (subtract)
Current liability increases = cash inflow (add)
Interest paid is operating; dividends paid is financing

tbs.title: Direct Method Cash Flows Reconciliation
tbs.scenario:
Metro Services Inc. is preparing its operating cash flows using the direct method. The following information is available:

Income Statement:
• Sales revenue: $2,400,000
• Cost of goods sold: $1,440,000
• Operating expenses: $560,000
• Depreciation expense (included in operating): $80,000
• Interest expense: $45,000
• Income tax expense: $95,000

Balance Sheet Changes:
• Accounts receivable increased: $60,000
• Inventory increased: $35,000
• Prepaid expenses decreased: $8,000
• Accounts payable increased: $25,000
• Accrued expenses decreased: $12,000
• Interest payable increased: $5,000
• Income taxes payable decreased: $15,000

requirements.question: Calculate cash received from customers.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Sales ($2,400,000) - AR increase ($60,000) = $2,340,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate cash paid to suppliers for inventory.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

COGS ($1,440,000) + Inventory increase ($35,000) - AP increase ($25,000) = $1,450,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate cash paid for operating expenses.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Operating expenses ($560,000) - Depreciation ($80,000) - Prepaid decrease ($8,000) + Accrued decrease ($12,000) = $484,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate cash paid for interest.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Interest expense ($45,000) - Interest payable increase ($5,000) = $40,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate total cash flows from operating activities (direct method).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Cash from customers ($2,340,000) - Cash to suppliers ($1,450,000) - Operating expenses ($484,000) - Interest ($40,000) - Taxes ($110,000) = $256,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Cash from customers = Sales ± AR change
Cash to suppliers = COGS ± Inventory ± AP
Remove non-cash items from expenses
Adjust for payable changes

tbs.title: Government Fund Accounting - Budget and Encumbrances
tbs.scenario:
The City of Oakville adopted its General Fund budget for fiscal year ending June 30, Year 1:

Adopted Budget:
• Estimated revenues: $5,000,000
• Appropriations: $4,800,000

During the year, the following transactions occurred:
1. January 15: Issued purchase order for police vehicles, $180,000
2. March 1: Received the vehicles, actual cost $175,000
3. March 15: Paid the vendor for the vehicles
4. April 1: Issued purchase order for office supplies, $25,000
5. June 30: Office supplies not yet received (still encumbered)

requirements.question: Record the entry to establish the budget at the beginning of the year.
Record the encumbrance for the police vehicles (January 15).
Record the receipt of police vehicles and reversal of encumbrance (March 1).
What is the total amount of encumbrances outstanding at June 30?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Only the office supplies ($25,000) remain encumbered at year-end.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

hints: Estimated Revenues is a debit (anticipating inflows)
Appropriations is a credit (authorizing outflows)
Encumbrances reserve unexpended appropriations

tbs.title: Government-Wide to Fund Statement Reconciliation
tbs.scenario:
The City of Riverside reports the following in its governmental funds:

General Fund Balance Sheet (June 30, Year 1):
• Total Fund Balance: $2,500,000

The city has the following items not reported in governmental funds:
• Capital assets, net of depreciation: $15,000,000
• General long-term debt: $8,000,000
• Accrued interest on long-term debt: $120,000
• Internal service fund net position (serves governmental): $400,000
• Deferred outflows (pension): $250,000
• Deferred inflows (pension): $180,000
• Net pension liability: $600,000

The city needs to prepare the reconciliation from fund balance to net position.

requirements.question: Prepare the reconciliation from fund balance to net position of governmental activities.
What is the net position of governmental activities?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$2,500,000 + $15,000,000 - $8,000,000 - $120,000 + $400,000 + $250,000 - $180,000 - $600,000 = $9,250,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

tbs.title: Revenue Recognition - Multiple Performance Obligations
tbs.scenario:
TechSolutions Inc. enters into a contract with Customer ABC on January 1, Year 1. The contract includes:
• Software license (perpetual) - Standalone selling price: $150,000
• Implementation services - Standalone selling price: $50,000  
• Two years of post-contract support (PCS) - Standalone selling price: $30,000 per year

Total contract price: $210,000
Payment terms: $100,000 due at signing, $110,000 due upon implementation completion

Additional facts:
• Implementation is completed on March 31, Year 1
• The software license is distinct and can be used without implementation
• PCS includes software updates and telephone support

requirements.question: How many performance obligations exist in this contract?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Three performance obligations: (1) Software license - distinct good, (2) Implementation services - distinct service, (3) PCS - distinct series of daily services (combined as one PO). Each is capable of being distinct and separately identifiable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the transaction price allocated to the software license using the relative SSP method.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Total SSP = $150,000 + $50,000 + $60,000 = $260,000. Software allocation = ($150,000 / $260,000) × $210,000 = $121,154

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Prepare the journal entry on January 1, Year 1 when the contract is signed.
What is the PCS revenue to be recognized in Year 1 (April 1 - December 31)?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

PCS allocation = ($60,000 SSP / $260,000 total SSP) × $210,000 transaction price = $48,462 total PCS revenue over 2 years. Year 1 (April-Dec): 9/24 × $48,462 = $18,173. Alternatively: $48,462 / 2 years = $24,231/year, then 9/12 × $24,231 = $18,173.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Step 1: Identify the contract(s) with the customer
Step 2: Identify the performance obligations (distinct goods/services)
Step 3: Determine the transaction price ($210,000)
Step 4: Allocate transaction price using relative SSP
Step 5: Recognize revenue when (or as) each PO is satisfied

tbs.title: Bank Reconciliation with Complex Adjustments
tbs.scenario:
Apex Manufacturing is preparing its bank reconciliation for December 31, Year 1.

Bank Statement Information:
• Ending balance per bank: $87,450
• Deposits in transit: $12,300
• Outstanding checks: $8,750
• Bank service charges: $45
• NSF check from customer: $1,200
• Interest earned: $85
• Bank error: Bank incorrectly charged Apex $500 for another company's check

Company Records:
• Cash balance per books before adjustments: $91,790
• Customer note collected by bank: $3,500 (includes $150 interest)
• Accounts receivable from note: $3,350

requirements.question: Calculate the adjusted bank balance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Bank balance $87,450 + Deposits in transit $12,300 - Outstanding checks $8,750 + Bank error $500 = $91,500

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the adjusted book balance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Book balance $91,790 - Service charges $45 - NSF check $1,200 + Interest earned $85 + Note collected $3,500 - Note receivable already recorded? Check facts. = Adjusted should equal bank $91,500

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Prepare the journal entry to record the adjustments to the book balance.

hints: Deposits in transit and outstanding checks adjust the BANK balance
Service charges, NSF checks, and note collections adjust the BOOK balance
Bank errors are bank adjustments
The adjusted bank and book balances must agree

tbs.title: Pension Accounting - PBO and Pension Expense
tbs.scenario:
Sterling Industries sponsors a defined benefit pension plan. The following information relates to the plan for Year 1:

Beginning balances (January 1, Year 1):
• Projected Benefit Obligation (PBO): $2,400,000
• Fair value of plan assets: $2,100,000
• Prior service cost (OCI): $180,000 (10-year remaining service period)

Year 1 activity:
• Service cost: $240,000
• Discount rate: 6%
• Expected return on plan assets: 7% (actual return was 8%)
• Company contribution: $350,000
• Benefits paid to retirees: $200,000
• Actuarial loss on PBO (new in Year 1): $45,000

requirements.question: Calculate the interest cost component of net periodic pension cost.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Interest cost = Beginning PBO × Discount rate = $2,400,000 × 6% = $144,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the expected return on plan assets.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Expected return = Beginning plan assets × Expected rate = $2,100,000 × 7% = $147,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the amortization of prior service cost for Year 1.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Amortization = Prior service cost / Remaining service period = $180,000 / 10 years = $18,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate net periodic pension cost (benefit) for Year 1.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Service cost $240,000 + Interest cost $144,000 - Expected return $147,000 + PSC amortization $18,000 = $255,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the ending PBO at December 31, Year 1.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Beginning PBO $2,400,000 + Service cost $240,000 + Interest cost $144,000 - Benefits paid $200,000 + Actuarial loss $45,000 = $2,629,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the funded status at December 31, Year 1.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Plan assets ending = Beginning $2,100,000 + Actual return ($2,100,000 × 8% = $168,000) + Contributions $350,000 - Benefits paid $200,000 = $2,418,000. Funded status = Plan assets - PBO = $2,418,000 - $2,629,000 = -$211,000 (underfunded). Note: The funded status represents the net pension liability to be reported on the balance sheet.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Net periodic pension cost = Service cost + Interest cost - Expected return + Amortization of PSC +/- Amortization of gains/losses
The difference between expected and actual return goes to OCI
PBO changes: +Service cost, +Interest cost, -Benefits paid, +/-Actuarial changes
Funded status = Fair value of plan assets - PBO (negative = underfunded)
